facts.quizQuestion: Which US state has the only one-syllable name?
facts.explanation: Maine is the only US state whose name has just one syllable. All 49 other state names have two or more syllables. Maine was admitted to the Union in 1820.
facts.statement: Maine is the only US state with a one-syllable name.
facts.wowFactor: Every single other US state name has at least two syllables — Maine stands alone with just one.

facts.quizQuestion: What wind speed in mph was recorded atop New Hampshire's Mount Washington in 1934?
facts.explanation: On April 12, 1934, observers atop Mount Washington recorded a wind gust of 231 mph (372 km/h), which stood as the world record for surface wind speed for 76 years until 2010. Mount Washington is known as 'Home of the World's Worst Weather.'
facts.statement: Mount Washington in New Hampshire recorded a wind speed of 231 mph in 1934 — a world record that stood for 76 years.
facts.wowFactor: A weather station on a New Hampshire mountain once clocked winds fast enough to launch a small plane — 231 mph, a world record held for 76 years.
facts.visualDescription: A snow-blasted mountain summit observation tower battered by furious winds, pixel art anemometer spinning wildly, ice formations clinging to instruments, dramatic storm clouds swirling.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Vermont become the first US territory to abolish slavery?
facts.explanation: Vermont's constitution of 1777 banned slavery, making it the first US territory to do so — 88 years before the 13th Amendment abolished slavery nationwide in 1865. Vermont was still an independent republic at the time, not yet a US state.
facts.statement: Vermont abolished slavery in 1777, making it the first US territory to do so — 88 years before the 13th Amendment.
facts.wowFactor: Vermont banned slavery in 1777 while the US was still fighting the Revolutionary War — nearly a century before Lincoln's era.
facts.visualDescription: A historic Vermont town hall with a dramatic declaration posted on the door, pixel art autumn maple trees in brilliant red and orange, rolling green hills, a quill pen and parchment scroll.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Harvard University founded, making it the oldest in the US?
facts.explanation: Harvard University was founded in 1636 in Cambridge, Massachusetts, making it the oldest institution of higher education in the United States. It was founded just 16 years after the Pilgrims landed at Plymouth Rock.
facts.statement: Harvard University, founded in 1636 in Massachusetts, is the oldest university in the United States.
facts.wowFactor: Harvard was founded in 1636 — it's older than the United States itself by 140 years.

facts.explanation: Boston is the capital and largest city of Massachusetts. It is one of the oldest cities in the US, founded in 1630, and played a central role in the American Revolution including the Boston Tea Party and Boston Massacre.
facts.statement: Boston is the capital of Massachusetts and one of the oldest cities in the United States.
facts.wowFactor: Boston is both the capital and the largest city of Massachusetts — one of the rare cases where they're the same place.

facts.quizQuestion: Which US state is the smallest by total area?
facts.explanation: Rhode Island covers just 1,545 square miles, making it the smallest US state by area. Despite its tiny size, it was one of the original 13 colonies and the first to declare independence from Britain in May 1776 — two months before the Declaration of Independence.
facts.statement: Rhode Island is the smallest US state by area, covering just 1,545 square miles.
facts.wowFactor: Rhode Island is so small that it could fit inside Alaska over 400 times — yet it was bold enough to declare independence from Britain two months before anyone else.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Connecticut adopt the Fundamental Orders, considered the world's first written constitution?
facts.explanation: Connecticut adopted the Fundamental Orders in 1639, widely regarded as the world's first written constitution establishing a government by consent of the governed. This is why Connecticut is nicknamed the 'Constitution State.'
facts.statement: Connecticut's Fundamental Orders of 1639 are considered the world's first written constitution, earning it the nickname 'Constitution State.'
facts.wowFactor: Connecticut wrote the world's first constitution in 1639 — 148 years before the US Constitution and long before the concept was fashionable.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the capital of New York State — not New York City?
facts.explanation: Albany, not New York City, is the capital of New York State. Albany has been a continuous seat of government since 1797 and is one of the oldest surviving European settlements in America, founded by Dutch traders in the early 1600s.
facts.statement: Albany is the capital of New York State — not New York City, which is the most populous city in the US.
facts.wowFactor: Millions of people assume New York City is the capital of New York — but the actual capital is Albany, a city few outsiders could find on a map.

facts.quizQuestion: The Statue of Liberty stands in the harbor of which US state?
facts.explanation: The Statue of Liberty stands on Liberty Island in New York Harbor. Though the island is geographically closer to New Jersey, it is jurisdictionally part of New York. The statue was a gift from France, dedicated in 1886.
facts.statement: The Statue of Liberty stands on Liberty Island in New York Harbor and is jurisdictionally part of New York State.
facts.wowFactor: The Statue of Liberty is technically in New York — but the island is physically closer to New Jersey, causing a legal border dispute that went all the way to the Supreme Court.

facts.explanation: New Jersey is the most densely populated state in the US, with approximately 1,263 people per square mile. Despite being only 8,723 square miles in size, it has nearly 9.3 million residents packed into the corridor between New York City and Philadelphia.

facts.quizQuestion: In which US state was the Declaration of Independence signed in 1776?
facts.explanation: The Declaration of Independence was signed in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania on August 2, 1776 (though voted upon on July 4). Pennsylvania also hosted the Constitutional Convention in 1787, making Philadelphia arguably the birthplace of the United States as a nation.

facts.wowFactor: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania hosted both the signing of the Declaration of Independence AND the Constitutional Convention — America was literally born in this one city.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Delaware become the first state to ratify the US Constitution?
facts.explanation: Delaware ratified the US Constitution on December 7, 1787, making it the first state to do so and earning it the nickname 'The First State.' It ratified unanimously, 30-0. Delaware Day is still celebrated annually on December 7.

facts.quizQuestion: The Star-Spangled Banner was written at Fort McHenry in which US state?
facts.explanation: Francis Scott Key wrote 'The Star-Spangled Banner' on September 14, 1814 while watching the British bombardment of Fort McHenry in Baltimore, Maryland during the War of 1812. The poem became the US national anthem in 1931.

facts.quizQuestion: Which US state holds the first presidential primary election every four years?
facts.explanation: New Hampshire holds the first presidential primary election in the US every four years, a tradition dating to 1920. By state law, New Hampshire must hold its primary before any similar contest, giving it outsized influence on which candidates survive to later contests.

variants.quizQuestion: Hoover Dam, which created Lake Mead, is located on the border of which two states?

variants.quizQuestion: Which US state is home to the mysterious Area 51 military facility?
variants.statement: Area 51, the classified US Air Force installation, is located in the Nevada desert near Groom Lake.

variants.quizQuestion: Which state's capital is Carson City, not Las Vegas?
variants.statement: Despite Las Vegas's fame, Nevada's capital is Carson City — a small city near Lake Tahoe.

variants.quizQuestion: Which is the driest state in the contiguous United States?
variants.statement: Nevada is the driest US state, with vast stretches of the Great Basin desert receiving fewer than 4 inches of rain annually.

facts.quizQuestion: Which state produces roughly one-third of all potatoes grown in the United States?
facts.explanation: Idaho's volcanic soil, warm days, and cool nights create ideal potato-growing conditions. The state produces about 13 billion pounds of potatoes annually — roughly one-third of the US total.
facts.statement: Idaho produces about one-third of all US potatoes, thanks to its rich volcanic soil and ideal mountain climate.
facts.wowFactor: Shoshone Falls in Idaho drops 212 feet — 36 feet higher than Niagara Falls — yet most Americans have never heard of it.
facts.visualDescription: Rolling green potato fields in a wide valley, dark volcanic soil visible at row edges, distant snow-capped peaks on the horizon.

variants.quizQuestion: Shoshone Falls, taller than Niagara Falls at 212 feet, is located in which state?
variants.statement: Shoshone Falls on the Snake River in Idaho drops 212 feet, 36 feet taller than Niagara Falls.

variants.quizQuestion: The star garnet, found only in Idaho and India, is the gem of which US state?
variants.statement: Idaho's state gem is the star garnet, a rare gemstone found in only two places on Earth: Idaho and India.

variants.quizQuestion: Craters of the Moon National Monument, covering 750,000 acres of lava fields, is in which state?
variants.statement: Craters of the Moon in Idaho preserves vast lava fields from eruptions that ended just 2,000 years ago.

variants.quizQuestion: Which state's nickname is the 'Gem State' due to its abundant mineral deposits?
variants.statement: Idaho is nicknamed the 'Gem State' — nearly every gemstone known has been found there, including opals, garnets, and jade.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the capital of Washington state — not Seattle?
facts.explanation: A common misconception is that Seattle is Washington's capital. The actual capital is Olympia, a smaller city at the southern tip of Puget Sound. Seattle is the largest city but not the capital.
facts.statement: Olympia is the capital of Washington state — a common misconception names Seattle, which is only the largest city.
facts.wowFactor: Washington is the only US state named after a president, and it causes constant confusion with Washington D.C. — locals often specify 'Washington State' or just 'the state.'
facts.visualDescription: Washington state capitol dome reflected in a calm harbor, Puget Sound stretching to forested Olympic Mountains in the background.

variants.quizQuestion: Washington is the only US state named after a president. Which president?
variants.statement: Washington state, admitted in 1889, is the only US state named after a US president.

variants.quizQuestion: Which state produces more apples than any other in the United States?
variants.statement: Washington state grows over 60% of all US apples, primarily in the Yakima and Wenatchee valleys.

variants.quizQuestion: Amazon, Microsoft, Boeing, and Starbucks are all headquartered in which US state?
variants.statement: Washington state is home to Amazon, Microsoft, Boeing, and Starbucks — an extraordinary concentration of global companies.

variants.quizQuestion: Mount Rainier, an active stratovolcano and the highest peak in the Cascades, is in which state?
variants.statement: Mount Rainier in Washington reaches 14,411 feet and is considered the most dangerous volcano in the US due to its proximity to Seattle.

facts.quizQuestion: Which state contains Crater Lake, the deepest lake in the United States at 1,949 feet?
facts.explanation: Crater Lake formed roughly 7,700 years ago when the volcano Mount Mazama erupted and collapsed. At 1,949 feet deep, it is the deepest lake in the US and is famous for its extraordinary blue color.
facts.statement: Crater Lake in Oregon, formed from a collapsed volcano, is the deepest lake in the United States at 1,949 feet.
facts.wowFactor: Crater Lake is so deep and clear that scientists can see objects at 100+ feet underwater — and its blue is so vivid it looks digitally enhanced in photos.
facts.visualDescription: Crater Lake's impossibly blue water inside a collapsed volcanic caldera, Wizard Island emerging from the center, pine forest ringing the rim.

variants.quizQuestion: Which state has no sales tax and is famous for the Oregon Trail wagon route?
variants.statement: Oregon has no sales tax, and the historic Oregon Trail terminated near Oregon City after its 2,000-mile route from Missouri.

variants.quizQuestion: Portland, Oregon's largest city, is known for which unofficial motto?
variants.statement: Portland, Oregon embraces its eclectic culture with the slogan 'Keep Portland Weird,' celebrating local businesses and counterculture.

variants.quizQuestion: Which state's capital is Salem, not Portland?
variants.statement: Oregon's capital is Salem, a mid-sized city in the Willamette Valley — Portland is the largest city but not the capital.

variants.quizQuestion: Which state was the 33rd admitted to the Union in 1859 along the Pacific coast?
variants.statement: Oregon became the 33rd state on February 14, 1859, and was the first state admitted on Valentine's Day.

facts.quizQuestion: If it were a country, which US state would have the world's 5th largest economy?
facts.explanation: California's GDP exceeds $3.6 trillion, which would rank it 5th in the world as an independent country — larger than the entire United Kingdom, France, or India. It is also the most populous US state.
facts.statement: California's economy, if it were a country, would rank 5th in the world — larger than the UK or France.
facts.wowFactor: California has the most people, the most cars, the most farms, the tallest trees, and the hottest recorded temperature in the entire United States.
facts.visualDescription: Silicon Valley office campuses stretching across a sunny valley, the San Francisco Bay glittering in the distance, redwood forests visible on hillsides.

variants.quizQuestion: Death Valley, the lowest and hottest point in North America, is in which state?
variants.statement: Death Valley, California sits 282 feet below sea level and recorded the highest reliably measured air temperature on Earth: 134°F in 1913.

variants.quizQuestion: The world's tallest trees, the coastal redwoods reaching over 380 feet, grow in which state?
variants.statement: California's coastal redwood forests contain the world's tallest living trees, some exceeding 380 feet.

variants.quizQuestion: Which US state is the most populous, with over 39 million residents?
variants.statement: California is the most populous US state, home to more than 39 million people — nearly twice as many as the second-most-populous state.

variants.quizQuestion: Which state's 1848 Gold Rush triggered the largest mass migration in US history?
variants.statement: The California Gold Rush of 1849 brought over 300,000 migrants from around the world, transforming a remote territory into the 31st state within two years.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the capital of California — not Los Angeles or San Francisco?
facts.explanation: Sacramento has been California's state capital since 1854. Despite Los Angeles and San Francisco being far more famous, the capital is Sacramento, located in the Central Valley.
facts.statement: Sacramento is the capital of California, not Los Angeles or San Francisco — a common misconception.
facts.wowFactor: Sacramento was the western terminus of the Pony Express in 1860 and a key hub during the Gold Rush — it was once the fastest-growing city in the world.
facts.visualDescription: California State Capitol building with its white dome and gold accents surrounded by manicured gardens, palm trees lining the approach.

variants.quizQuestion: Which California city served as the western terminus of the Pony Express in 1860?
variants.statement: Sacramento was the western endpoint of the Pony Express mail route, receiving messages from St. Joseph, Missouri in about 10 days.

variants.quizQuestion: California joined the Union in 1850 as which numbered state?
variants.statement: California was admitted as the 31st state on September 9, 1850, just over a year after the Gold Rush began.

variants.quizQuestion: Mount Whitney, the highest peak in the contiguous United States, is in which state?
variants.statement: Mount Whitney at 14,505 feet is the highest point in the lower 48 states, located in California's Sierra Nevada mountains.

variants.quizQuestion: Hollywood, the center of the US film and television industry, is in which state?
variants.statement: Hollywood, a neighborhood of Los Angeles, California, has been the heart of American film production since the early 1900s.

facts.quizQuestion: Which state was purchased from Russia in 1867 for $7.2 million, mocked as 'Seward's Folly'?
facts.explanation: Secretary of State William Seward negotiated the purchase of Alaska from Russia in 1867 for $7.2 million — about 2 cents per acre. Critics called it 'Seward's Folly' or 'Seward's Icebox,' but it proved to be one of history's greatest land deals.
facts.statement: Alaska was purchased from Russia in 1867 for $7.2 million — about 2 cents per acre — in a deal mocked as 'Seward's Folly.'
facts.wowFactor: Alaska is simultaneously the northernmost, westernmost, AND easternmost US state — its Aleutian Islands cross the 180° meridian into the Eastern Hemisphere.
facts.visualDescription: A vast Alaskan wilderness panorama with the aurora borealis swirling green and purple above snow-capped peaks and a frozen river below.

variants.quizQuestion: Alaska is the northernmost, westernmost, AND easternmost US state. Why easternmost?
variants.correctAnswer: Its Aleutian Islands cross the 180th meridian
variants.statement: Alaska's Aleutian Island chain extends across the 180° meridian into the Eastern Hemisphere, making it technically the easternmost US state.

variants.quizQuestion: Juneau, Alaska's capital, is unique among state capitals for what reason?
variants.correctAnswer: It cannot be reached by road
variants.statement: Juneau is the only US state capital that cannot be reached by road — it is accessible only by air or sea.

variants.quizQuestion: Denali, the highest peak in North America at 20,310 feet, is in which state?
variants.statement: Denali in Alaska reaches 20,310 feet, making it the highest mountain on the North American continent.

variants.quizQuestion: Which state has more coastline than all other US states combined?
variants.statement: Alaska's coastline, including its many islands and inlets, exceeds the combined coastline of all other US states at over 33,000 miles.

facts.quizQuestion: Alaska is approximately how many times larger than the second-largest state, Texas?
facts.explanation: Alaska covers 663,268 square miles compared to Texas's 268,596 square miles — Alaska is about 2.5 times the size of Texas. Many people underestimate Alaska's size because of how maps distort high-latitude landmasses.
facts.statement: Alaska is approximately 2.5 times the size of Texas, the second-largest US state.
facts.wowFactor: If you superimposed Alaska on the lower 48 states, it would stretch from Florida to California and from Texas to Minnesota.
facts.visualDescription: A scale comparison map showing Alaska's enormous silhouette overlaid on the contiguous United States, dwarfing states below.

variants.quizQuestion: Alaska was admitted as the 49th state in which year?
variants.statement: Alaska was admitted to the Union on January 3, 1959, becoming the 49th state and the first non-contiguous US state.

variants.quizQuestion: Which US state is by far the largest by land area?
variants.statement: Alaska covers 663,000 square miles — more than the next three largest states combined.

variants.quizQuestion: Alaska borders which country to the east?
variants.statement: Alaska shares its eastern border entirely with Canada, separated from the contiguous US by British Columbia and Yukon Territory.

variants.quizQuestion: What is Alaska's capital city, accessible only by air or sea?
variants.statement: Juneau is Alaska's capital and the only US state capital completely inaccessible by road.

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the only US state made entirely of islands?
facts.explanation: Hawaii consists of 137 islands spread across 1,500 miles of the Pacific Ocean. It was admitted as the 50th and final US state in 1959, the same year as Alaska.
facts.statement: Hawaii is the only US state made entirely of islands, consisting of 137 islands in the Pacific Ocean.
facts.wowFactor: Hawaii has no native land snakes — none — making it one of the few inhabited places on Earth where you can hike for days without any snake risk whatsoever.
facts.visualDescription: A dramatic Hawaiian island landscape with a black lava shoreline meeting turquoise Pacific waves, volcanic peaks rising in tropical mist above.

variants.quizQuestion: Which US state has no native land snakes of any species?
variants.statement: Hawaii is one of the few inhabited landmasses on Earth with no native land snake species — snakes are actually illegal to own there.

variants.quizQuestion: Pearl Harbor, site of the 1941 Japanese attack that brought the US into WWII, is in which state?
variants.statement: Pearl Harbor naval base in Oahu, Hawaii was attacked on December 7, 1941, prompting the US to enter World War II the following day.

variants.quizQuestion: Which US state is the most isolated population center on Earth?
variants.statement: Hawaii is the most geographically isolated population center on Earth, located 2,400 miles from the US mainland.

variants.quizQuestion: Which is the only US state that commercially grows coffee?
variants.statement: Hawaii is the only US state where coffee is grown commercially, with Kona coffee from the Big Island being world-renowned.

facts.quizQuestion: In which state did the Wright Brothers achieve the first powered flight at Kitty Hawk in 1903?
facts.explanation: The Wright Brothers flew their powered aircraft at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina on December 17, 1903 — the first sustained, controlled heavier-than-air flight. They chose the site for its steady winds and soft sand. Source: Wikipedia/Wright_Brothers
facts.statement: The Wright Brothers achieved the first powered flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina in 1903.
facts.wowFactor: The Wright Brothers' first flight lasted only 12 seconds and covered just 120 feet — shorter than a Boeing 747 is long!
facts.visualDescription: A fragile wooden biplane lifts off sandy dunes at dawn, two figures in caps watching as wind whips across the Outer Banks under a pale winter sky

variants.question: What historic aviation event happened at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina in 1903?
Why did the Wright Brothers choose Kitty Hawk for their first flight attempt?
The Wright Brothers' first flight at Kitty Hawk lasted over one minute.
The Wright Brothers achieved the first powered flight at ___, North Carolina.

facts.quizQuestion: What is the capital of North Carolina?
facts.explanation: Raleigh is the capital of North Carolina, though Charlotte is the state's largest city. Raleigh is part of the Research Triangle along with Durham and Chapel Hill. Source: Wikipedia/North_Carolina
facts.statement: Raleigh is the capital of North Carolina.
facts.wowFactor: Raleigh is named after Sir Walter Raleigh, who funded the lost colony of Roanoke — one of America's oldest unsolved mysteries.
facts.visualDescription: A stately capitol dome rises above oak trees in a charming Southern city, research universities visible in the distance forming a triangle of knowledge

variants.question: Raleigh is the capital of which US state?
Which North Carolina city is part of the Research Triangle with Durham and Chapel Hill?
Charlotte is the capital of North Carolina.
The capital of North Carolina is ___, not Charlotte.

facts.quizQuestion: Which state's nickname 'The Golden State' comes from the 1849 Gold Rush?
facts.explanation: California earned the nickname 'The Golden State' after the 1848-1855 Gold Rush, which brought 300,000 people to the territory and led to California becoming a state in 1850. Source: Wikipedia/California_Gold_Rush
facts.statement: California is called 'The Golden State' because of the 1849 Gold Rush.
facts.wowFactor: The California Gold Rush was so massive that San Francisco's population exploded from 200 to 36,000 in just two years!
facts.visualDescription: Prospectors panning for gold in a rushing Sierra Nevada stream, golden nuggets glinting in the pan as covered wagons line the trail behind them

variants.question: What event gave California its 'Golden State' nickname?
The 1849 Gold Rush brought 300,000 people to which US state?
California became a state directly because of the Gold Rush.
California is called 'The _____ State' after the 1849 Gold Rush.

facts.quizQuestion: Which is the only US state with no native land snakes?
facts.explanation: Hawaii has no native land snakes because it is so isolated in the Pacific Ocean that snakes never naturally colonized the islands. Importing snakes is illegal. Source: Wikipedia/Hawaii
facts.statement: Hawaii is the only US state with no native land snakes.
facts.wowFactor: Hawaii is so serious about staying snake-free that importing a snake can result in a $200,000 fine and up to 3 years in prison!
facts.visualDescription: Lush tropical Hawaiian island paradise with palm trees, colorful birds, and vibrant flowers but conspicuously zero snakes anywhere in the jungle

variants.question: What is unusual about Hawaii's wildlife compared to all other US states?
Why does Hawaii have no native land snakes?
variants.correctAnswer: Too isolated for snakes to reach

variants.question: It is illegal to import snakes into Hawaii.
Hawaii is the only US state with no native land ___.

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Texas join the US after being an independent republic?
facts.explanation: Texas was an independent nation called the Republic of Texas from 1836 to 1845, after winning independence from Mexico. It joined the US as the 28th state in 1845. Source: Wikipedia/Republic_of_Texas
facts.statement: Texas was an independent republic from 1836 to 1845 before joining the United States.
facts.wowFactor: Texas is one of only two US states that were independent countries before joining the Union — the other is Hawaii!
facts.visualDescription: A Lone Star flag flying proudly over a dusty frontier town, transitioning to an American flag as cowboys ride beneath the vast Texas sky

variants.question: What was Texas before it became a US state in 1845?
Which two US states were once independent countries before joining the Union?
Texas was an independent country for about 9 years before joining the US.
Texas was an independent ___ from 1836 to 1845.

facts.quizQuestion: Which state has more coastline than all other US states combined?
facts.explanation: Alaska has over 33,904 miles of tidal coastline — more than all other US states combined. This is due to its incredibly jagged shoreline with thousands of islands, fjords, and inlets. Source: Wikipedia/Alaska
facts.statement: Alaska has more coastline than all other US states combined.
facts.wowFactor: Alaska's coastline is so vast that if you straightened it out, it would stretch from New York to Tokyo and back — twice!
facts.visualDescription: An endless jagged coastline stretching to the horizon, thousands of icy fjords and islands carved by glaciers under the midnight sun of Alaska

variants.question: Alaska holds which geographic record among US states?
Why does Alaska have such an enormous coastline?
Florida has more coastline than Alaska.
Alaska has more ___ than all other US states combined.
